result:
The rain doesn’t fall in straight lines here. It never has. It drifts sideways like it’s carrying gossip between buildings, slapping against the glass of my eighth-floor window in irregular, conspiratorial bursts. I’m watching it now, laptop balanced on my knees, half-drunk coffee going cold beside me. The city outside is a watercolor left in the rain—neon bleeding into concrete, headlights stretching into long gold ribbons that dissolve the moment you try to focus on them. Everything feels like it’s dissolving lately. Maybe that’s a good thing.

I’ve been thinking about memory again. Not the tidy, scrapbook kind. The feral kind. The kind that wakes you at 3:17 a.m. with the exact smell of your grandmother’s hallway in 1997—wood polish, old books, and something faintly like oranges that you could never quite name. Memory doesn’t ask permission. It just shows up wearing the face of someone you haven’t thought about in twenty years and says, Remember when you were nine and convinced the moon followed you home every night? Remember how certain you were that if you ran fast enough you could slip between seconds?

I used to believe that. Still do, some days.

There’s a peculiar freedom in getting older that no one warns you about. When you’re young, freedom feels like rebellion—staying out until your curfew collapses, dyeing your hair terrible colors, kissing people whose names you’ll forget. Later, freedom becomes something quieter and more ferocious: the freedom to stop performing. To admit you like the soft rock station sometimes. To cry at commercials about dogs. To say "I don’t know" without feeling like a fraud. To write badly and not apologize for it. To love things openly even when they’re unfashionable. To become, in small ways, the person your sixteen-year-old self would have found terminally uncool.

I’ve been trying to become terminally uncool. It’s harder than it sounds.

Yesterday I caught myself dancing in the kitchen to a song from 1994 while making toast. Not the self-conscious sway of someone who knows they’re being watched. Real dancing. The kind with elbows. The kind where your hips do whatever they want and your shoulders have opinions. For three glorious minutes I was not a functioning adult with taxes and opinions about grout. I was just meat and rhythm and ridiculous joy. The toast burned. Worth it.

There’s a bird that lives on my fire escape. I don’t know what kind it is—some scruffy brown thing with ambitions. Every morning it throws itself at the glass like it’s personally offended by the concept of windows. Bang. Pause. Bang. It’s been doing this for eleven weeks. I’ve named it Gary. Gary and I have an understanding: he will never get through the glass, and I will never stop leaving him sunflower seeds like a pathetic suitor. Some relationships are built on better foundations. This one is built on delusion and shelled snacks. It works for us.

I’ve been reading too much lately. Not the gentle, uplifting kind. The kind that cracks you open like a geode and leaves you staring at all your ugly glittering parts. Books that make you want to call your mother and apologize for things you did at seventeen. Books that make grocery lists feel like moral failures. There’s something masochistic about it, but also something like coming home. The best books don’t tell you what to think. They just sit beside you while you remember who you actually are under all the performance and coping mechanisms and carefully cultivated internet personas.

My internet persona is very confident about fermentation, by the way. In real life I have killed approximately nine sourdough starters and counting. They keep looking at me with their sad little bubbles like, Why do you do this to us? I don’t know, Kevin. I don’t know why I ruin everything I touch with affection. Maybe that’s the human condition. We reach out with clumsy hands and say I love you by trying to keep you alive and somehow that translates to nine dead yeast colonies and a refrigerator full of regret.

The rain has changed tempo. It’s softer now, almost polite. The city lights smear less aggressively across the wet streets. Everything feels like it’s breathing out after holding its breath too long. I like this version of the city—the one that admits it’s tired. Most days it wears its exhaustion like armor. Tonight it’s just tired. There’s a difference.

I’ve been thinking about how we’re all just elaborate defense mechanisms wearing skin suits. Every personality quirk, every hobby, every political opinion, every “I’m not like other girls” declaration—it’s all armor. Some of it’s beautiful armor. Some of it’s armor made of Christmas lights and bad decisions. But it’s armor. The trick isn’t to stop wearing it. That’s impossible. The trick is to become the kind of person who knows they’re wearing it. To wave at other people across the battlefield of daily life like, Nice breastplate. Is that hand-forged irony or did you buy it pre-distressed?

I don’t want to reach the end of my life and realize I was just really good at being a believable simulation of a person. I want to be so obviously, embarrassingly myself that it makes other people uncomfortable in the best way. The way that makes them go home and take their own armor off for five minutes just to remember what their actual shoulders feel like.

Gary just slammed into the window again. Legend.

There’s a cat sleeping on my feet that I don’t technically own. She belongs to the neighbor but has apparently decided my apartment has better vibes. I let her. She judges my life choices with extreme prejudice but also purrs like a broken lawnmower when I read to her, so we have an understanding. Her name is Susan. Susan does not care about my word count or my existential spiraling. Susan wants chin scratches and for me to stop moving my feet. Fair enough.

I think the rain is stopping. Or maybe it’s just getting quieter so it can listen to me think. Cities do that sometimes. They get attentive. You can feel it—like the whole metropolis is leaning in, saying, Go on. I’m listening. Tell me what it feels like to be temporary meat that somehow learned how to cry at sunsets and fall in love with songs in minor keys.

It feels like almost too much, if I’m honest. It feels like being handed a Stradivarius when all you know how to play is “Hot Cross Buns.” But you play it anyway. You make the ugly beautiful noises because the alternative is silence, and silence is the only real death while you’re still walking around.

The coffee is completely cold now. The words have spilled out like they were waiting behind a dam that finally cracked. I don’t know if any of this makes sense. I don’t know if it’s supposed to. Maybe writing is just another way of throwing yourself against the glass like Gary, demanding to be let into some brighter, warmer room where all the scattered parts of you finally fit together into something that doesn’t feel like it’s apologizing for existing.

Maybe the room was never locked. Maybe the glass was never there. Maybe we’ve just been slamming ourselves against the idea of separation for so long we’ve started to believe the impact is the point.

I don’t know.

But the rain stopped, and Susan is purring, and somewhere out there Gary is probably already planning tomorrow’s assault on my window. The city is breathing. I’m breathing. The words are breathing too, now that they’re out.

That feels like enough for tonight.

(Word count: 1002)

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
